The Indian culture is very much "do as I say".  

With a heavy presence in India you are working odd hours to talk to your Indian teammates who are wonderful to work with but theres a big time zone gap so you have to work earlier and later than normal, but the company is trying to force you to go to the office but when will you get there is the big question because you are working odd hours.
  
The company keeps saying the strategy is to "grow" which tells you everything you need to know about the company leadership; very few people even know what the strategy is.  

Despite all of this, the company asks for more from employees but they wont get more, people are very unhappy and very unwilling to work hard given theres zero incentive to do so from a compensation perspective.

pros: On paper the role looks exciting and fulfilling. Health insurance is good. Can take career in a lot of directions given the size of the organization. Surprising number of talented people stick around in NA and EMEA. A good place to work if you are based in India. Can hide if you find a pocket.
cons: Way behind the market on most criteria. India-based benchmarks mean the bar for what quality looks like is very low and the business today will likely be replaced by AI in a few years. You can feel the pressure/stress in the business as they cut all investments and delay or eliminate raises (especially for senior folks who they hope will take the hint and leave). Employee experience is transactional and treating people as commodities no matter how unique their skill set is. The culture is full of competitive “leaders” (use that word to define their position not the skill set) who steal work and credit from each other instead of working together. Works on a barter / favors system and you have to be “in the club” to get ahead (have heard it called the India mafia). Policies are overly complex and black hole operations are debilitating when you need to get stuff done. No way to get exceptions so if you are delivering outside of tech implementation work, you won’t be successful. Layers and layers of approvals - zero trust in leaders to make contextual decisions. Constant layoffs as they move everyone off shore to India so they can increase margins and give clients lower skilled talent for the same money. Have lost many discrimination lawsuits about this as the #1 H1B Visa provider in the US.  Becoming lower-tiered Infosys with the influx of leaders from that company since Ravi joined, most of whom are over their heads and unable to grasp the complexity of the business but fit the low-bar ethical and ethnicity mold. Used to talk about DEI but have failed in attracting or retaining women - most now being exited and numbers dropping quickly.

title: In my experience: - Okay place to start career. - Decent benefits and job security working in healthcare sector. - If you are willing to learn, you can learn, but it may not be exactly what you think you signed up for. - Starting with a cohort of new grads from all over the US and being able to share and experience new perspectives can be enjoyable. - Based on project you are on, there is a fair amount of downtime then things usually ramp up around release cycles. - If you like working on different types of projects, it is a consulting environment, so a great place for it. - Based on project, work with relevant technologies like (.NET, C#, SQL Server, Angular, etc) - Pays the bills
pros:
In my experience:
- Okay place to start career.
- Decent benefits and job security working in healthcare sector.
- If you are willing to learn, you can learn, but it may not be exactly what you think you signed up for.
- Starting with a cohort of new grads from all over the US and being able to share and experience new perspectives can be enjoyable.
- Based on project you are on, there is a fair amount of downtime then things usually ramp up around release cycles.
- If you like working on different types of projects, it is a consulting environment, so a great place for it.
- Based on project, work with relevant technologies like (.NET, C#, SQL Server, Angular, etc)
- Pays the bills
cons:
In my experience:
- Subpar pay, subpar bonuses, and unclear promotion process.
- Work can feel ambiguous at times.
- When in the office, playing the corporate game is a part of the process if you want to try your luck at getting on a more “desirable” team.
- Working with offshore teammates can be difficult for both parties. Timezones are vastly different for IST (end of their day) and MDT/MST (start of my day).
- Processes tend to move pretty slowly across the board.
- Strict adherence to "Scaled Agile" and processes over everything.
- Seems to be a lot of churn and burn.
- You are really just a number here.

Note: This feedback is based on my experience. There are learning opportunities, but it’s important to go in with eyes open.

pros: They're too large of a company to keep a close eye on you, so at least you can mostly do your work for your client.
cons:
The prior company I worked for was bought by Cognizant during the pandemic. It took Cognizant several years to on-board us, and when they finally did, they considered us "new employees" despite how long we'd been with our previous company. Because of this, we had no banked PTO at the start, and any progress towards job promotions were now irrelevant. 

Company makes no effort to assist you in finding your next project. They instruct you to apply through their internal job portal, as if you're applying for an external job anyways. Some random workers do submit project leads on your behalf, but I'm convinced they just do this to meet quotas because none of the jobs even remotely match your listed skill sets.

They only reach out to you when they actively need something, and often it's to tell you that your just assigned learning task is due tomorrow. Even then your name is but 1 of hundreds in the included excel doc.

They're primarily based in India, so email communications and meeting invites tend to be sent around 3 AM.

title: It's difficult to think of one.
pros: It's difficult to think of one.
cons:
After being absorbed by Cognizant, we were assured that no one would be at risk of losing their jobs and that, if necessary, they would find us other positions within the company. However, during my time there, employees were laid off in waves. If you want to feel like just a number on a spreadsheet rather than a valued individual, this is the place for you. I was among those laid off, and shortly after, I received an email from Cognizant stating they had found my information on a job board and thought I would be a good fit for their company. This demonstrated just how disorganized and, at times, heartless the company truly is.

Some of their policies were also quite unreasonable, such as requiring 48-hour notice before calling in sick if you didn’t want your absence counted against you. The hiring process was equally nightmarish, feeling more like applying for a government position than a corporate job. Many of my coworkers had their verification documents repeatedly rejected for who knows what reason. It’s worth noting again that we had been absorbed by Cognizant and were not new hires.

I have worked for questionable companies in the past, but Cognizant stands out as the worst place I’ve ever been employed. It's perplexing that they've received awards for being a great workplace when, during my time there, we were treated like nothing more than sheep.

Cognizant handles business process outsourcing for other technology companies. What I didn't understand at the time of being hired is even though I was a Cognizant employee, my employment was at the whim of the client we were providing services to. Because everything from the employment terms, hourly rates, benefits, etc were defined in the contract, there's not a lot that can be negotiated and there's not a lot of room for career growth. Projects may also end abruptly. In this case, you're put on the bench to find another position within the company. It's extremely generous because this time is fully paid with benefits. However, you're limited to projects/positions open at that time, which may/may not be a good fit. I got the impression that the only way to land a more permanent position is to stick it out and work here for 10+ years.

cons:
There is a lack of projects for North American (US) consultants, which could be blamed on the economy. However, there is an organizational shift happening now that is allowing offshore consultants (India) to apply for onshore (US) projects. Onshore (US) employees are not eligible to work any global projects, as far as I understand it. This means the competition for US-based workers has risen astronomically, while offshore consultants are able to get the projects. 

In addition, there is a period of time where a consultant can be on the "bench" for only 1 month, then Cognizant fires/lays off the employee. Keep in mind, there is no severance with this and it is not up to the consultant. If you worked for the company for 5 years and were on the bench for 1 month, apparently your time on the bench counts for your "severance". There has been no official communication about any of this, other than the push for offshore employees to get projects onshore. The company did not give any raises or bonuses within the past year, but the Executives are making a tremendous amount of money that is published online.

All of this combined has created an extremely tense work environment with prejudice and extreme competition. It is unclear what the long-term goal is for the company, especially with executive leadership always coming and going. This company does not seem like it will succeed in the long run.

I feel like this job would be perfect for someone who understands themselves, the industry, and the relationship between themselves and the industry well enough to self-direct and reach their own goals, but I was fresh out of college for this job.

Critical View: The constant shift between different projects and industries can result in a lack of depth in expertise and a feeling of being a jack-of-all-trades, master of none. Critical View: The company’s large size can sometimes make individual contributions feel insignificant, and it may be challenging to stand out in such a vast organization. Competitive Compensation and Benefits Pro: Cognizant generally offers competitive salaries and a comprehensive benefits package, which can be appealing to potential employees. Critical View: Despite competitive compensation, the demanding nature of consulting work often requires long hours, making it difficult to maintain a work-life balance. Access to Cutting-Edge Technologies Pro: Consultants at Cognizant often have access to the latest technologies and tools, which can be beneficial for professional growth. Critical View: Keeping up with rapid technological changes and learning new tools can be exhausting and stressful, especially under tight project deadlines. Opportunities for International Assignments Pro: The chance to work on international assignments can be exciting and provide valuable cultural experiences. Critical View: Frequent travel or relocation can be disruptive to personal life and lead to a lack of stability, which may not suit everyone. Variety of Projects and Industries

Critical View: The constant shift between different projects and industries can result in a lack of depth in expertise and a feeling of being a jack-of-all-trades, master of none.
Strong Brand and Market Presence

Critical View: The company’s large size can sometimes make individual contributions feel insignificant, and it may be challenging to stand out in such a vast organization.
Competitive Compensation and Benefits

Pro: Cognizant generally offers competitive salaries and a comprehensive benefits package, which can be appealing to potential employees.
Critical View: Despite competitive compensation, the demanding nature of consulting work often requires long hours, making it difficult to maintain a work-life balance.
Access to Cutting-Edge Technologies

Pro: Consultants at Cognizant often have access to the latest technologies and tools, which can be beneficial for professional growth.
Critical View: Keeping up with rapid technological changes and learning new tools can be exhausting and stressful, especially under tight project deadlines.
Opportunities for International Assignments

Pro: The chance to work on international assignments can be exciting and provide valuable cultural experiences.
Critical View: Frequent travel or relocation can be disruptive to personal life and lead to a lack of stability, which may not suit everyone.
Networking Opportunities

cons:
High Workload and Pressure

Con: Consultants often face high workloads and intense pressure to meet client expectations and project deadlines, which can lead to stress and burnout.
Critical View: The high-pressure environment can negatively impact mental and physical health, and the expectation to consistently deliver high performance can be unrealistic.
Limited Work-Life Balance

Con: The demanding nature of consulting work often results in long hours, including nights and weekends, making it difficult to achieve a work-life balance.
Critical View: The lack of flexibility and personal time can lead to job dissatisfaction and burnout, affecting overall quality of life.
Bureaucratic and Hierarchical Structure

Con: Cognizant’s large, hierarchical structure can lead to bureaucracy and slow decision-making processes, which can be frustrating for employees.
Critical View: Navigating the bureaucracy can be time-consuming and demotivating, particularly when trying to implement new ideas or drive change.
Unpredictable Job Security

Con: Job security can be uncertain, especially during economic downturns or organizational restructuring, leading to frequent layoffs and job instability.
Critical View: The lack of job stability can create anxiety and stress among employees, making it difficult to plan for the future.
Frequent Changes in Project Assignments

Con: Consultants may be required to frequently change projects or clients, which can disrupt continuity and create a lack of job satisfaction.
Critical View: The constant shift in assignments can make it challenging to develop long-term relationships with clients or to see the long-term impact of one's work.
High Expectations for Performance

Con: There is a high expectation for consultants to consistently deliver exceptional results, which can be challenging and stressful.
Critical View: The relentless pursuit of excellence can lead to a culture of overwork and unrealistic expectations, negatively affecting morale and job satisfaction.
Limited Career Advancement for Some

Con: Career advancement may be limited for some employees, particularly if they are not aligned with key projects or high-profile clients.
Critical View: The perception of favoritism or lack of transparency in promotion processes can lead to frustration and decreased motivation.
Intense Competition and High Turnover

Con: The competitive nature of consulting leads to high turnover rates, which can create a sense of job instability and make it difficult to build a cohesive team.
Critical View: The constant influx and outflux of talent can disrupt team dynamics and continuity, making it challenging to maintain long-term project quality.
Challenges in Maintaining Work Relationships

Con: The project-based nature of consulting can make it difficult to maintain long-term relationships with colleagues, as teams are often reshuffled.
Critical View: The lack of stable team relationships can lead to a sense of isolation and reduce opportunities for mentorship and collaboration.
